Name: Talisman's Special Solutions
Description: The NTWF (Talisman's Special Solutions Workflow) ontology from Jessica Talisman's 'Intentional Arrangement' series, modeled as a relational rulebook. One curated worked example — the Production Deployment Workflow — exercises every class, property, and competency question: three disjoint agent types (human / AI / pipeline), role-vs-agent separation, a delegation/escalation chain, an approval gate as a step subtype, step-to-step transitive ordering, a PROV provenance chain over artifacts, and DCAT dataset consumption. Every competency question is answered by a derived column or a transitive-closure view (no sidecar code); a small set of load-bearing INDEX/MATCH lookups resolves the role-to-agent and gate-to-role-to-approver chains the questions depend on.

Workflows.Description: Table: Workflows. The NTWF Workflow class — prov:Plan + schema:CreativeWork. Each workflow has Dublin Core metadata (title, description, identifier, created, modified), a lifecycle status from the SKOS scheme, and a collection of WorkflowSteps (ntwf:hasStep).
Workflows.summary_rich: The **root entity** of the NTWF demo. The single curated workflow — *Production Deployment* — carries full Dublin Core metadata, a SKOS lifecycle status, and six ordered steps. This is the table that shows how a slug (`Name`) is derived from a display name, and how a rollup (`CountOfNonProposedSteps`) counts related rows without any application code.

schema.name: RelativePath
schema.Description: Stable, DAG-derived location for this Workflow row. Root segment 'workflows' + the row's primary key. No leading slash so the Iri swap is a clean 1:1 substitution. The relational analogue of a REST resource path; unique by construction across the whole model.

schema.name: Iri
schema.Description: Opaque stable identifier (the dash-form of RelativePath). Because RelativePath has no leading slash, this is a clean SUBSTITUTE of '/' for '-'. The OWL transpiler mints each individual's IRI from this value (erb:<Iri>), so identity is path-derived and globally unique — no cross-table primary-key collisions.

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Short machine-friendly name for the workflow. Used for programmatic reference and URL slug generation.
schema.explanation_rich: **Your first calculated field.** `Name` is just `LOWER(DisplayName)` with spaces swapped for dashes — the kind of slug you'd usually write by hand in a controller. Here it lives in the rulebook. Worked example: the *Production Deployment* workflow has `DisplayName = "Production Deployment"`, so `Name` becomes `production-deployment`. Rename it to *Production Deployment v2* and the slug becomes `production-deployment-v2` everywhere — Postgres view, Python class, Excel cell — without touching a single line of code.

schema.name: Title
schema.Description: Human-readable title of the workflow. Maps to dct:title from Dublin Core. Example: 'Production Deployment Workflow'.

schema.name: Description
schema.Description: Detailed description of the workflow's purpose and scope. Maps to dct:description from Dublin Core. Should explain what business goal the workflow achieves.

schema.name: Identifier
schema.Description: External system identifier for cross-referencing. Maps to dct:identifier from Dublin Core. This is the join key back to document management systems, ticket systems, or other operational systems.

schema.name: Modified
schema.Description: Last modification timestamp. Maps to dct:modified from Dublin Core. Critical for answering CQ5: 'Which workflows haven't been reviewed or updated in twelve months?'

schema.name: Created
schema.Description: Creation timestamp. Maps to dct:created from Dublin Core. Records when the workflow was first defined.

schema.name: StalenessThresholdMonths
schema.Description: The governance POLICY (in months): the full review cadence after which this workflow's compliance documentation is formally out of date. The docs go stale exactly when this review age is exceeded — IsStale fires the instant MonthsSinceModified passes this policy line, with no deferral. The article hardcodes the CQ5 question at twelve months ('which workflows haven't been reviewed in twelve months'); promoting that threshold to a raw, editable field makes the policy itself a fact in the SSoT rather than a constant buried in the IsStale formula — so an org can set a 6-month or 18-month review cadence and the staleness verdict recomputes. Defaults to 12 to match the article.

schema.name: WorkflowStatus
schema.Description: FK to WorkflowStatusConcepts. Captures the current lifecycle state of the workflow (draft, active, deprecated, archived). Maps to the SKOS CBox status vocabulary.

schema.name: WorkflowSteps
schema.Description: Reference to workflow steps. Represents the ntwf:hasStep relationship linking workflows to their constituent steps.

schema.name: CountOfNonProposedSteps
schema.Description: Calculated count of workflow steps in this workflow. Useful for workflow complexity analysis and reporting.
schema.explanation_rich: **Your first rollup.** This counts every `WorkflowSteps` row whose `Workflow` foreign key points back at this workflow's `WorkflowId`. No JOIN to write. Worked example: the *Production Deployment* workflow has five steps (`prod-deploy-step-1` through `prod-deploy-step-5`), so `CountOfNonProposedSteps` is `5`. Add or delete a step and the count updates on the next read.

schema.name: HasMoreThan1Step
schema.explanation_rich: **A second-order calculation.** This field reads another calculated field — `CountOfNonProposedSteps` — and turns it into a boolean. It's the smallest possible example of a DAG: a rollup feeds into a rule. Worked example: *Production Deployment* has 5 steps, so `HasMoreThan1Step` is `TRUE`. A hypothetical single-step workflow would return `FALSE`.

schema.name: CountAISteps
schema.Description: Number of steps in this workflow executed by an AIAgent (rollup over WorkflowSteps.IsExecutedByAI). This is the 'AI-executed' half of the article's CQ3 ('which steps are executed by AI agents') — counted against AIAgent individuals specifically, not the deterministic AutomatedPipeline, which is a disjoint agent type. Also drives the business-payoff query (a workflow is a compliance risk when an AI agent runs a step). Worked example: 2 (the AI risk-assessment step and the AI post-deployment health report).

schema.name: CountHumanSteps
schema.Description: Number of steps executed by a HumanAgent (rollup over WorkflowSteps.IsExecutedByHuman). The 'who actually runs this step' count — distinct from CountHumanRequiredSteps, which counts steps that demand a human decision (requiresHumanApproval). Worked example: 2 (the legal-review step and the release approval gate).

schema.name: CountHumanRequiredSteps
schema.Description: Number of steps that require a human decision (rollup over WorkflowSteps.RequiresHumanApproval). This is the 'human-required' half of the article's CQ3 ('which require a human decision'), answered — as the article notes — by a single FILTER on requiresHumanApproval. Worked example: 2 (the legal-review step and the release approval gate).

schema.name: CountApprovalConsistencyViolations
schema.Description: Number of steps that require human approval but are not human-filled (rollup over WorkflowSteps.ApprovalConsistencyViolation). The clean ABox witness: this is 0 for the Production Deployment workflow. A non-zero value is the relational signal of a Suite-4 consistency violation.
schema.explanation_rich: **A consistency invariant, rolled up.** Every step computes whether it violates the human-approval rule; this counts the violations for the whole workflow. A clean ABox holds it at **0** — the same green/red signal the article's reasoner produces, available as a single column with no reasoner in the loop.

schema.name: HasConsistencyViolation
schema.Description: TRUE iff at least one step breaks the human-approval consistency rule (CountApprovalConsistencyViolations > 0). The boolean witness of model integrity: a clean ABox holds it FALSE. This is what makes a broken rule a first-class input to the compliance verdict — a workflow with any consistency violation cannot be COMPLIANT.
schema.explanation_rich: **The integrity bit.** Where `CountApprovalConsistencyViolations` is the headcount, this is the yes/no: does the model currently contradict itself? It mirrors `HasAIAgentStep`/`IsOverTimeBudget` so the verdict can fold all three risk inputs the same way. The point: there is now an *implicit no-broken-rules rule* — any structural inconsistency forces the verdict to AT RISK, so 'COMPLIANT with a broken rule' is impossible by construction.

schema.name: HasAIAgentStep
schema.Description: TRUE iff at least one step in this workflow is executed by an AIAgent. The structural half of the article's business payoff query.

schema.name: MonthsSinceModified
schema.Description: Whole months since this workflow was last modified (dct:modified), measured live against NOW(). Drives CQ5 staleness. NOW() is seeded deterministically during conformance so test answers stay stable.

schema.name: IsStale
schema.Description: TRUE iff the workflow's compliance documentation is past its review policy — i.e. the review age in months exceeds the policy line: MonthsSinceModified > StalenessThresholdMonths. With the default the docs go stale at 12 months. Staleness fires the instant the review comes due — there is no renewal window or deferral. This is the article's CQ5 condition ('which workflows haven't been reviewed in twelve months') stated directly against the editable policy field.

schema.name: IsStaleAndHasAIAgent
schema.Description: The article's headline business question, as one boolean: a workflow that is BOTH stale (not reviewed in 12 months) AND has an AI-executed step — the highest compliance risk. Joins the metadata layer (dct:modified) with the accountability layer (filledBy → AIAgent) the way the closing SPARQL demo does, but as a single derived column.
schema.explanation_rich: **The whole article, in one cell.** The closing demonstration is a SPARQL query that crosses three layers — metadata (`dct:modified`), structure (`hasStep`), and accountability (`filledBy` → `AIAgent`) — to surface stale workflows running AI steps. Here that business question is a derived boolean: `IsStale AND HasAIAgentStep`. The substrate has already done the join; the answer is a column.

schema.name: CountDerivationLinks
schema.Description: Number of prov:wasDerivedFrom links among this workflow's artifacts (rollup over WorkflowArtifacts.HasDerivationParent). Answers the lineage half of CQ4: 5 artifacts form a chain with 4 derivation links.

schema.name: CountLegalOwnedSteps
schema.Description: Number of steps in this workflow whose owning department is Legal (rollup over WorkflowSteps.IsLegalOwned). CQ7: exactly one Legal-owned step in the Production Deployment workflow.

schema.name: CountEngineeringOwnedSteps
schema.Description: Number of steps whose owning department is Engineering (rollup over WorkflowSteps.IsEngineeringOwned). Feeds CQ7's Engineering-involvement check.

schema.name: InvolvesEngineeringAndLegal
schema.Description: TRUE iff this workflow has at least one Engineering-owned step AND at least one Legal-owned step. Answers CQ7 ('which workflows involve both engineering and legal') as a single boolean — the Production Deployment workflow qualifies (4 Engineering steps + 1 Legal step).

schema.name: CountInferredPrecedencePairs
schema.Description: Number of step-ordering pairs that the transitive closure of ntwf:precedesStep INFERRED (rollup over the closure view vw_step_precedence_closure where is_inferred = TRUE). The article's signature count: 6 of the 10 closure pairs were never asserted — including step-1 -> step-5. NOTE: this single-workflow model has exactly one Workflow, so the global closure view is wholly this workflow's; the COUNTIFS is unfiltered because every precedence edge belongs to the Production Deployment DAG.
schema.explanation_rich: **The inference, counted.** Four edges (1->2->3->4->5) were written down; the `closure` field type materialized `vw_step_precedence_closure` and derived the full ordering. This rolls up the rows that carry `is_inferred = TRUE` — the pairs no human asserted, the article's '6 inferred' figure, surfaced as a column. Delete the view and `effortless build` regenerates the same 6.

schema.name: CountAssertedPrecedencePairs
schema.Description: Number of step-ordering pairs that were directly ASSERTED as ntwf:precedesStep edges (rollup over vw_step_precedence_closure where is_inferred = FALSE) — the hop-1 rows. The article's 4 asserted edges. Together with CountInferredPrecedencePairs (6) this sums to the 10-pair closure, making CountOfPrecedenceClosurePairs an honest asserted+inferred total rather than an unconditional count. Single-workflow note as on CountInferredPrecedencePairs: the global closure view is this workflow's.

schema.name: CountOfPrecedenceClosurePairs
schema.Description: Total number of step-ordering pairs in the transitive closure of ntwf:precedesStep = asserted (4) + inferred (6) = 10. The article's headline closure cardinality, witnessing that the 4 asserted edges over a 5-step chain close to all 10 (i<j) pairs. Computed as CountAssertedPrecedencePairs + CountInferredPrecedencePairs so the total is provably the sum of the two halves, not a separate unconditional view count that could silently drift from them.
schema.explanation_rich: **The closure size, as a single number.** A 5-step linear chain has C(5,2) = 10 ordering pairs; the article asserts 4 of them and lets the reasoner derive the rest. Here `CountAssertedPrecedencePairs + CountInferredPrecedencePairs` = 4 + 6 = **10**, the same total the OWL-RL closure produces — and because it is the literal sum of the asserted and inferred witnesses, it cannot disagree with them.

schema.name: CountRolesWithBadFillerCardinality
schema.Description: Number of roles that do NOT have exactly one filledBy arm set (rollup over Roles.HasExactlyOneFiller = FALSE). The three agent classes are owl:disjointWith one another and ntwf:filledBy is functional, so a clean ABox has 0 such roles — this is the Suite-1 functional/disjointness witness as a single integer. A non-zero value is the relational signal of the Suite-4 disjointness violation (a role filled by two agent classes, or by none). NOTE: this single-workflow model has exactly one Workflow and every Role participates in it, so the count is over all roles; a multi-workflow model would scope it through a role→workflow path.
schema.explanation_rich: **Disjointness + functionality, counted to zero.** In OWL, a role typed as filled by both a `HumanAgent` and an `AIAgent` is an inconsistency the reasoner catches via `owl:disjointWith`; a role filled by none violates the functional `filledBy`. Relationally, every role must have exactly one of its three filler arms set — `HasExactlyOneFiller`. This rolls those up: **0** in the clean ABox, the same green signal the reasoner produces, with no reasoner in the loop.

schema.name: CountAgentTypeChanges
schema.Description: Number of filledBy assignment periods that changed the agent CLASS of a role (rollup over RoleAssignments.IsAgentTypeChange = TRUE). Part IV distinguishes a same-class personnel/model swap from an agent-type transition; this counts the latter. NOTE: single-workflow model — every Role participates in the one workflow, so the count is over all assignment history; a multi-workflow model would scope it through a role→workflow path.
schema.explanation_rich: **Agent-type transitions, counted.** Part IV's governance distinction: swapping one AI model for another (same class) is routine, but changing a role from AI execution to human execution (or vice versa) is a different kind of event. `RoleAssignments.IsAgentTypeChange` flags each such transition; this rolls them up for the workflow.

schema.name: CountComplianceAuditChanges
schema.Description: Number of filledBy assignment periods that took a previously AI-executed binding and reassigned it to a human (rollup over RoleAssignments.RequiresComplianceAudit = TRUE). Part IV: 'Changing the agent type of a step from ntwf:AIAgent to ntwf:HumanAgent ... is a data operation with compliance implications.' Each such row must carry when (ValidFrom) and why (Reason). NOTE: single-workflow scoping as above.
schema.explanation_rich: **The compliance-flagged transitions, counted.** Part IV singles out AIAgent -> HumanAgent reassignment as the case the audit record must capture (when + why). `RoleAssignments.RequiresComplianceAudit` fires on exactly that direction; this rollup surfaces how many such transitions a workflow's role history contains — the audit queue, as a number.

data.Title: Production Deployment Workflow
data.Description: The primary NTWF ABox example. A 5-step workflow for deploying software to production at Talisman's Special Solutions. Steps involve a human Release Manager (step 1), an AI Risk Analysis Agent (step 2), a human Legal Compliance Reviewer at an Approval Gate (step 3), an automated CI/CD pipeline (step 4), and the Release Manager again for the post-deployment report (step 5). Demonstrates all three agent types, the delegation chain, artifact provenance, and DCAT dataset consumption.

WorkflowSteps.Description: Table: WorkflowSteps. The NTWF WorkflowStep class — prov:Activity. Each step is first-class and individually addressable, belongs to one Workflow (ntwf:isStepOf), and is assigned to exactly one Role (ntwf:assignedRole). Step-to-step ordering is modeled in the StepPrecedence junction; the ApprovalGate subtype specializes a step via a 1:1 FK.
WorkflowSteps.summary_rich: The **building blocks**. Each step belongs to one `Workflow` and is assigned to one `Role` (never directly to an agent — that's Heuristic 2). Some steps require a human to approve (`RequiresHumanApproval`); others run automatically. Step 3 is specialized by an `ApprovalGate`. Step order is recorded as first-class `StepPrecedence` edges, not as a magic integer.

schema.name: ParentPath
schema.Description: Helper: the Workflows parent's RelativePath, pulled across the Workflow FK. Exists so RelativePath can concatenate the '/steps/' segment using only local-field '&' concat (the transpiler compiles a lookup as a pure passthrough, not a lookup+concat).

schema.name: RelativePath
schema.Description: Stable, DAG-derived location: this row nests under its Workflows parent. Concatenates the parent's path (ParentPath) with '/steps/' + this row's primary key. The DAG performs the recursion — one hop per table via ParentPath — so the full ancestry is encoded without a recursive formula. Unique by construction.

schema.name: Iri
schema.Description: Opaque stable identifier (the dash-form of RelativePath). Because RelativePath has no leading slash, this is a clean SUBSTITUTE of '/' for '-'. The OWL transpiler mints each individual's IRI from this value (erb:<Iri>), so identity is path-derived and globally unique — no cross-table primary-key collisions.

schema.name: Workflow
schema.Description: Foreign key to the parent workflow. Represents the inverse of ntwf:hasStep, enabling navigation from step to its containing workflow (ntwf:isStepOf).

schema.name: SequencePosition
schema.Description: Integer ordinal position of the step within its workflow. Maps to ntwf:sequencePosition, declared as owl:FunctionalProperty (each step has exactly one position). Enables positional queries. Note: this is a display/positional convenience — the authoritative ordering lives in the StepPrecedence edges.

schema.name: AssignedRole
schema.Description: Foreign key to the Role responsible for executing this step. Maps to ntwf:assignedRole (owl:FunctionalProperty — exactly one role per step). Critical for implementing Heuristic 2 (role-agent separation): steps point to roles, not directly to agents.

schema.name: RequiresHumanApproval
schema.Description: Boolean flag indicating whether a human agent must fill the assigned role. Maps to ntwf:requiresHumanApproval. Enables answering CQ3: 'Which steps require human decisions vs. AI execution?'

schema.name: StepDurationMinutes
schema.Description: Expected duration of this step in minutes. Maps to ntwf:stepDurationMinutes (datatype property, not functional). Enables SLA and throughput analysis.

schema.name: ConsumesDataset
schema.Description: FK to Datasets. Records which DCAT dataset this step consumes as input. Kept separate from artifact consumption to preserve DCAT metadata semantics (consumesDataset vs. requiresArtifact).

schema.name: ProducesArtifacts
schema.Description: Back-reference to WorkflowArtifacts produced by this step. Inverse of WorkflowArtifacts.ProducedByStep (ntwf:producesArtifact / prov:wasGeneratedBy).

schema.name: RequiresArtifacts
schema.Description: FK to WorkflowArtifact(s) this step CONSUMES as input. Maps to ntwf:requiresArtifact (aligned to prov:used). Kept distinct from producesArtifact (prov:generated) and from consumesDataset (dcat:Dataset) so the input/output and artifact/dataset semantics stay separate. Inverse is WorkflowArtifacts.RequiredBySteps.

schema.name: ApprovalGate
schema.Description: Back-reference to the ApprovalGate subtype row that specializes this step, if any. Inverse of ApprovalGates.WorkflowStep. A step has zero or one approval gate; when present, the gate adds escalationThresholdHours and marks the step as a blocking decision checkpoint. This models ntwf:ApprovalGate rdfs:subClassOf WorkflowStep as a shared-key 1:1 specialization rather than collapsing two DAG nodes into one.

schema.name: Precedes
schema.Description: Back-reference to StepPrecedence edges where this step is the FromStep (the predecessor). Inverse of StepPrecedence.FromStep. Together with PrecededBy, lets you walk the ntwf:precedesStep ordering in both directions.

schema.name: PrecededBy
schema.Description: Back-reference to StepPrecedence edges where this step is the ToStep (the successor). Inverse of StepPrecedence.ToStep. Part of the ntwf:precedesStep transitive ordering relationship.

schema.name: ExecutingHumanAgent
schema.Description: The HumanAgent (if any) that executes this step, resolved through ntwf:assignedRole → ntwf:filledBy (the HumanAgent arm). Load-bearing lookup: it follows the role→agent indirection the article relies on, so a step knows its executing agent without per-step agent bindings.

schema.name: ExecutingAIAgent
schema.Description: The AIAgent (if any) that executes this step, resolved through ntwf:assignedRole → ntwf:filledBy (the AIAgent arm). One of the three polymorphic filledBy arms.

schema.name: ExecutingAutomatedPipeline
schema.Description: The AutomatedPipeline (if any) that executes this step, resolved through ntwf:assignedRole → ntwf:filledBy (the AutomatedPipeline arm).

schema.name: ExecutingAgentType
schema.Description: Which of the three disjoint agent classes executes this step (HumanAgent / AIAgent / AutomatedPipeline), derived from whichever filledBy arm the assigned role has set. Answers the typing half of CQ3 ('which steps are executed by AI agents, and which require a human decision').
schema.explanation_rich: **The role→agent indirection, resolved to a type.** The step points at a role; the role is filled by exactly one of a human, an AI, or a pipeline. This field reads the three lookup arms and reports the executing agent's class — the relational stand-in for the OWL `rdf:type` of the `filledBy` target. In the Production Deployment workflow this yields two AI/pipeline steps and three human steps.

schema.name: IsExecutedByAI
schema.Description: TRUE when this step's assigned role is filled by an AIAgent. Feeds CQ3 and the business payoff query (stale workflows with AI-executed steps).

schema.name: IsExecutedByHuman
schema.Description: TRUE when this step's assigned role is filled by a HumanAgent. Feeds CQ3's human-vs-AI step split.

schema.name: ApprovalConsistencyViolation
schema.Description: Detectable-error witness: TRUE iff this step requires human approval (RequiresHumanApproval) yet its assigned role is NOT filled by a HumanAgent. In the OWL ABox this is the rule that only a HumanAgent may fill a role on a requiresHumanApproval step; a clean ABox yields FALSE for every step. This is the relational equivalent of the Suite-4 disjointness/consistency check.
schema.explanation_rich: **An inconsistency made visible.** The article's reasoner flags it when a non-human fills a role on a step that requires human approval. Here the same violation surfaces as a boolean: `RequiresHumanApproval = true AND ExecutingHumanAgent is blank`. Every step in the clean Production Deployment ABox returns FALSE — flip an approval step onto an AI-filled role and this lights up, exactly as the reasoner would.

schema.name: ApprovalIsHumanFilled
schema.Description: Positive form of the human-only-gate rule: TRUE iff this step's human-approval obligation is satisfied — either the step does not require human approval (vacuously satisfied), or it does and its assigned role is filled by a HumanAgent. The clean Production Deployment ABox yields TRUE for every step. This is the affirmative complement of ApprovalConsistencyViolation: the two are always opposite when approval is required, and this one is additionally TRUE on steps that need no approval.
schema.explanation_rich: **The gate rule, as a satisfied-yes boolean.** `ApprovalConsistencyViolation` asks 'is this step broken?'; this asks 'is this step's approval obligation met?'. When `RequiresHumanApproval` is false the obligation is vacuously met (TRUE); when true, it is met only if a human actually fills the role. Every step in the clean ABox returns TRUE — the green half of the same Suite-4 invariant the article's reasoner enforces.

schema.name: OwningDepartment
schema.Description: The department that owns this step's assigned role, resolved through AssignedRole → Roles.OwnedBy. Lets a workflow report which departments its steps touch (CQ7: 'which workflows involve both Engineering and Legal, and at what steps do they intersect').

schema.name: IsLegalOwned
schema.Description: TRUE iff this step's owning department is Legal. Rolls up to CQ7's count of Legal-owned steps (exactly one in the Production Deployment workflow).

schema.name: IsEngineeringOwned
schema.Description: TRUE iff this step's owning department is Engineering. Rolls up to CQ7's Engineering-involvement check.

ApprovalGates.Description: Table: ApprovalGates. The NTWF ApprovalGate class — rdfs:subClassOf WorkflowStep. Modeled as a class-table-inheritance subtype: each gate row shares identity with exactly one WorkflowStep (via the WorkflowStep 1:1 FK) and carries only the gate-specific attribute, escalationThresholdHours. The step it specializes keeps the common attributes (requiresHumanApproval, assigned role, etc.). This preserves the article's double-typing — a gate IS a step — without collapsing two DAG nodes into one.

schema.name: ParentPath
schema.Description: Helper: the WorkflowSteps parent's RelativePath, pulled across the WorkflowStep FK. Exists so RelativePath can concatenate the '/approval-gates/' segment using only local-field '&' concat (the transpiler compiles a lookup as a pure passthrough, not a lookup+concat).

schema.name: RelativePath
schema.Description: Stable, DAG-derived location: this row nests under its WorkflowSteps parent. Concatenates the parent's path (ParentPath) with '/approval-gates/' + this row's primary key. The DAG performs the recursion — one hop per table via ParentPath — so the full ancestry is encoded without a recursive formula. Unique by construction.

schema.name: Iri
schema.Description: Opaque stable identifier (the dash-form of RelativePath). Because RelativePath has no leading slash, this is a clean SUBSTITUTE of '/' for '-'. The OWL transpiler mints each individual's IRI from this value (erb:<Iri>), so identity is path-derived and globally unique — no cross-table primary-key collisions.

schema.name: WorkflowStep
schema.Description: 1:1 FK to the WorkflowStep this gate specializes (subtype shared key). This is the relational expression of ntwf:ApprovalGate rdfs:subClassOf WorkflowStep: the gate row adds escalationThresholdHours to its step. Inverse is WorkflowSteps.ApprovalGate.

schema.name: EscalationThresholdHours
schema.Description: Integer number of hours that may elapse on a pending gate before the ntwf:delegatesTo chain activates. Maps to ntwf:escalationThresholdHours. Domain applies only to ApprovalGate individuals — which is exactly why the gate is its own subtype table and this attribute does not live on every WorkflowStep.

schema.name: GateRole
schema.Description: The role responsible for this gate's underlying step, resolved through WorkflowStep → WorkflowSteps.AssignedRole. First hop of the CQ2 chain (gate → role → approver).

schema.name: GateApproverHuman
schema.Description: The human agent who approves at this gate, resolved through the two-hop chain gate → GateRole → Roles.FilledByHumanAgent. Answers CQ2 ('who is responsible for approving a production deployment') directly: the release-approval gate resolves to the Release Manager role, filled by Maria Gonzalez.
schema.explanation_rich: **CQ2 answered by a lookup chain.** A gate specializes a step; the step has a role; the role is filled by a person. This field walks that chain — `GateRole` (gate → step → role) then `Roles.FilledByHumanAgent` (role → human) — to name the approver. For the release-approval gate the answer is `ntwf-maria-gonzalez`, exactly what the article's SPARQL returns.

StepPrecedence.Description: Table: StepPrecedence. The NTWF ntwf:precedesStep ordering relationship, modeled as a first-class step-to-step junction. Each row is one directed edge: FromStep precedes ToStep. ntwf:precedesStep is an owl:TransitiveProperty — the four asserted edges (1->2, 2->3, 3->4, 4->5) imply the full closure of ten ordering pairs (including 1->5, which is never asserted). Each edge is a first-class node in the DAG, never a 'helper' integer.

schema.name: ParentPath
schema.Description: Helper: the WorkflowSteps parent's RelativePath, pulled across the FromStep FK. Exists so RelativePath can concatenate the '/precedence/' segment using only local-field '&' concat (the transpiler compiles a lookup as a pure passthrough, not a lookup+concat).

schema.name: RelativePath
schema.Description: Stable, DAG-derived location: this row nests under its WorkflowSteps parent. Concatenates the parent's path (ParentPath) with '/precedence/' + this row's primary key. The DAG performs the recursion — one hop per table via ParentPath — so the full ancestry is encoded without a recursive formula. Unique by construction.

schema.name: Iri
schema.Description: Opaque stable identifier (the dash-form of RelativePath). Because RelativePath has no leading slash, this is a clean SUBSTITUTE of '/' for '-'. The OWL transpiler mints each individual's IRI from this value (erb:<Iri>), so identity is path-derived and globally unique — no cross-table primary-key collisions.

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Human-readable edge label derived from its endpoints. Mirrors the FromStep -> ToStep direction.

schema.name: FromStep
schema.Description: FK to the predecessor WorkflowStep — the step that comes BEFORE. The source of the ntwf:precedesStep edge. Inverse is WorkflowSteps.Precedes.

schema.name: ToStep
schema.Description: FK to the successor WorkflowStep — the step that comes AFTER. The target of the ntwf:precedesStep edge. Inverse is WorkflowSteps.PrecededBy.

schema.name: PrecedesStepClosure
schema.Description: Transitive closure of ntwf:precedesStep (an owl:TransitiveProperty). The 4 asserted edges (1→2, 2→3, 3→4, 4→5) imply the full 10-pair ordering closure — including the never-asserted step-1 → step-5. Materialized by the transpiler as the view vw_step_precedence_closure(from_id, to_id, hop_distance, is_inferred): 4 asserted (hop 1) + 6 inferred rows. This is the article's headline inference made to fire, not seeded.
schema.explanation_rich: **The transitive closure, computed by the substrate.** `ntwf:precedesStep` is declared `owl:TransitiveProperty`: assert `1→2, 2→3, 3→4, 4→5` and a reasoner derives all ten ordering pairs. Here the `closure` field type emits a cycle-safe `WITH RECURSIVE` view, `vw_step_precedence_closure`, that does exactly that — every reachable `(from_id, to_id)` pair with `hop_distance` (shortest derivation) and `is_inferred` (TRUE when no direct edge asserts the pair). Worked example: `step-1 → step-5` appears with `hop_distance = 4`, `is_inferred = true`, though it was never written down. Delete the view and `effortless build` regenerates it identically.

Roles.Description: Table: Roles. The NTWF Role class — a custom root with no adequate standard match, declared disjoint with WorkflowStep and WorkflowArtifact. Roles are the heart of Heuristic 2 (role-agent separation): WorkflowSteps point to Roles; Roles point to exactly one agent (human, AI, or pipeline) via the polymorphic filledBy relationship. When personnel or models change, one filledBy triple changes and the workflow structure is untouched.

schema.name: RelativePath
schema.Description: Stable, DAG-derived location for this Role row. Root segment 'roles' + the row's primary key. No leading slash so the Iri swap is a clean 1:1 substitution. The relational analogue of a REST resource path; unique by construction across the whole model.

schema.name: Iri
schema.Description: Opaque stable identifier (the dash-form of RelativePath). Because RelativePath has no leading slash, this is a clean SUBSTITUTE of '/' for '-'. The OWL transpiler mints each individual's IRI from this value (erb:<Iri>), so identity is path-derived and globally unique — no cross-table primary-key collisions.

schema.name: Label
schema.Description: Human-readable display name. Maps to rdfs:label. Per Heuristic 6: if you cannot write a clear label, you do not yet understand the concept well enough to model it.

schema.name: Comment
schema.Description: Detailed description of the role's responsibilities and scope. Maps to rdfs:comment. Should define what the role covers, what it excludes, and how it differs from adjacent roles.

schema.name: HasCapability
schema.Description: FK to AgentCapabilityConcepts. Declares the capability this role requires of its filler. Maps to ntwf:hasCapability. Enables CQ: 'Which roles require AI-specific capabilities?'

schema.name: FilledByHumanAgent
schema.Description: One arm of the polymorphic ntwf:filledBy relationship: FK to the HumanAgent that fills this role today. Exactly one of FilledByHumanAgent / FilledByAIAgent / FilledByAutomatedPipeline is set per role (filledBy is functional; the three agent types are owl:disjointWith each other). Roles whose capability requires human judgment or legal review must use this arm.

schema.name: FilledByAIAgent
schema.Description: One arm of the polymorphic ntwf:filledBy relationship: FK to the AIAgent that fills this role today. Exactly one filledBy arm is set per role. An AIAgent may fill probabilistic-capability roles (e.g. risk analysis) but never a role whose step has requiresHumanApproval.

schema.name: FilledByAutomatedPipeline
schema.Description: One arm of the polymorphic ntwf:filledBy relationship: FK to the AutomatedPipeline that fills this role today. Exactly one filledBy arm is set per role. Pipelines fill deterministic execution roles (e.g. CI/CD).

schema.name: OwnedBy
schema.Description: Foreign key to the Department that owns this role. Maps to ntwf:ownedBy (owl:FunctionalProperty). Enables answering CQ7: 'Which workflows involve both Engineering and Legal?'

schema.name: DelegatesTo
schema.Description: Foreign key to the next Role in the escalation chain. Maps to ntwf:delegatesTo (traversable via the SPARQL property path delegatesTo+). Enables answering CQ6: 'What happens when the Release Manager / VP of Engineering is unavailable?'

schema.name: WorkflowSteps
schema.Description: Back-reference to workflow steps assigned to this role. Inverse of WorkflowSteps.AssignedRole.

schema.name: FromDelegatesTo
schema.Description: Back-reference: the Role that delegates TO this role (one step up the escalation chain). Inverse of Roles.DelegatesTo.

schema.name: RoleAssignments
schema.Description: Back-reference to the temporal filledBy history for this role (every validity period, current and retained). Inverse of RoleAssignments.Role.

schema.name: DelegationClosure
schema.Description: Transitive closure of ntwf:delegatesTo over the self-referential DelegatesTo FK. The asserted escalation edges (Release Manager → VP Engineering, VP Engineering → CTO) imply the never-asserted reachability Release Manager → CTO. Materialized as vw_roles_closure(from_id, to_id, hop_distance, is_inferred). This is the SQL equivalent of the SPARQL delegatesTo+ property path.
schema.explanation_rich: **The escalation chain, closed transitively.** `ntwf:delegatesTo` chains Release Manager → VP Engineering → CTO. The article traverses it with the SPARQL property path `delegatesTo+`; here the `closure` field type emits `vw_roles_closure` from the self-referential `DelegatesTo` FK. Release Manager → CTO surfaces as an inferred row (`hop_distance = 2`, `is_inferred = true`) even though only the two single-step edges were asserted — answering CQ6 without leaving the database.

schema.name: FilledByArmCount
schema.Description: Number of polymorphic ntwf:filledBy arms set on this role (of FilledByHumanAgent / FilledByAIAgent / FilledByAutomatedPipeline). Should always be exactly 1 — mirroring filledBy being functional and the three agent types being mutually disjoint.

schema.name: HasExactlyOneFiller
schema.Description: Disjointness/functional witness: TRUE iff exactly one filledBy arm is set. The three agent classes are owl:disjointWith one another and ntwf:filledBy is functional, so a clean ABox has this TRUE for every role. Setting two arms (a role filled by both a human and an AI) is the Suite-4 disjointness violation — here it flips this to FALSE.
schema.explanation_rich: **Disjointness, enforced as a boolean.** In OWL, typing one individual as both `HumanAgent` and `AIAgent` is an inconsistency the reasoner catches via `owl:disjointWith`. Relationally, the equivalent is 'a role must be filled through exactly one of its three arms'. `FilledByArmCount = 1` for every role in the clean ABox; set a second arm and this witness fails — the same error, surfaced without a reasoner.

schema.name: FillerType
schema.Description: Which disjoint agent class fills this role (HumanAgent / AIAgent / AutomatedPipeline), from whichever filledBy arm is set. Lets the delegation-chain query confirm CQ6's 'zero AI agents in the escalation chain'.

data.Label: CI/CD Executor
data.Comment: Executes the automated build, test, and deployment sequence. Filled by an AutomatedPipeline. Demonstrates CQ3: this step does NOT require human approval.

data.Label: Chief Technology Officer
data.Comment: Top of the delegation chain. Has cap-executive-authorization. When both Release Manager and VP Engineering are unavailable, the CTO is the final escalation target.

data.Label: Deployment Health Agent
data.Comment: Generates the post-deployment health report by summarizing telemetry after a release. A role, not an identity — currently filled by the DeploymentHealth-AI agent (health-summarizer-v1.2.0), it could be reassigned to a human or a newer model without changing the workflow structure. Its RoleAssignments history records an earlier human-reviewer period and the agent-type transitions.

data.Label: VP of Engineering
data.Comment: First escalation target when Release Manager is unavailable. Demonstrates ntwf:delegatesTo chain: Release Manager → VP Engineering → CTO.

data.Label: Risk Analysis Agent
data.Comment: Performs probabilistic risk scoring over the Q1 risk metrics dataset. This is a role, not an identity — filled by RiskAnalysis-AI today but could be replaced by a newer model version without changing the workflow structure.

data.Label: Legal Compliance Reviewer
data.Comment: Certifies that the deployment plan meets regulatory and contractual requirements. Must be a human agent — AI agents may not fill this role (requiresHumanApproval).

data.Label: Release Manager
data.Comment: Owns the production deployment process. Coordinates all parties, triggers the pipeline, and signs the final post-deployment report.

RoleAssignments.Description: Table: RoleAssignments. The temporal history of ntwf:filledBy. Part IV (Change Management) requires that when a filledBy triple is updated the old triple is NOT deleted — it is timestamped and retained, or replaced with a versioned triple carrying a validity period. Each row is one filledBy binding with a ValidFrom / ValidTo validity period and the reason for the change, so that 'which agent was executing this step on March 1, 2026?' is answerable from the graph. The current binding on Roles.FilledBy* is the row whose ValidTo is blank (IsCurrent = TRUE); closed rows preserve provenance and chain of custody. This is the relational equivalent of Part IV's named-graph / versioned-triple retention practice.

schema.name: ParentPath
schema.Description: Helper: the Roles parent's RelativePath, pulled across the Role FK. Exists so RelativePath can concatenate the '/assignments/' segment using only local-field '&' concat.

schema.name: RelativePath
schema.Description: Stable, DAG-derived location: this assignment nests under its Role parent. Concatenates the parent's path (ParentPath) with '/assignments/' + this row's primary key. Unique by construction.

schema.name: Iri
schema.Description: Opaque stable identifier (the dash-form of RelativePath). The OWL transpiler mints each individual's IRI from this value (erb:<Iri>), so identity is path-derived and globally unique.

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Human-readable label for this assignment period: the role and the validity window.

schema.name: Role
schema.Description: FK to the Role this assignment binds an agent to. The subject of the historical ntwf:filledBy triple.

schema.name: FilledByHumanAgent
schema.Description: One arm of the polymorphic filledBy binding for this assignment period: FK to the HumanAgent who filled the role during this window. Exactly one filler arm is set per assignment.

schema.name: FilledByAIAgent
schema.Description: One arm of the polymorphic filledBy binding: FK to the AIAgent who filled the role during this window. Exactly one filler arm is set per assignment.

schema.name: FilledByAutomatedPipeline
schema.Description: One arm of the polymorphic filledBy binding: FK to the AutomatedPipeline that filled the role during this window. Exactly one filler arm is set per assignment.

schema.name: ValidFrom
schema.Description: Start of the validity period for this filledBy binding (inclusive). Part IV: a retained/versioned triple 'carries the validity period'. ISO date.

schema.name: ValidTo
schema.Description: End of the validity period for this filledBy binding (exclusive). Blank means the binding is still current — this is the live ntwf:filledBy value mirrored on Roles. A non-blank value means the binding was superseded; the row is retained (not deleted) to preserve provenance.

schema.name: Reason
schema.Description: The WHY of the change, per Part IV: 'the audit record must reflect when that transition happened and why.' e.g. 'initial assignment', 'departure / backfill', 'model upgrade', 'compliance reassignment to human'.

schema.name: PriorFillerType
schema.Description: The agent class (HumanAgent / AIAgent / AutomatedPipeline) of the binding this assignment SUPERSEDED, or blank for the first assignment of a role. Lets the agent-type-change audit (Part IV: AIAgent -> HumanAgent) be witnessed without re-deriving from the prior row.

schema.name: FillerType
schema.Description: Which agent class filled the role during this period, derived from the three filler arms. Mirrors Roles.FillerType but for the historical binding.

schema.name: IsCurrent
schema.Description: TRUE iff this is the live binding (ValidTo is blank). The set of IsCurrent rows reproduces exactly the current Roles.FilledBy* values; the rest are retained history. Part IV: 'the old triple should not be deleted' — closed rows stay, only IsCurrent flips.
schema.explanation_rich: **History without deletion.** Part IV's change-management rule is that updating `ntwf:filledBy` must not discard the old triple — it is timestamped and retained. `IsCurrent` is the boolean that marks the one live binding per role (`ValidTo` blank); every superseded binding remains in the table as a closed validity period, so the chain of custody survives.

schema.name: WasActiveAsOfAuditDate
schema.Description: Part IV's signature temporal query: 'which agent was executing this step on March 1, 2026?'. TRUE iff this binding's validity period contains 2026-03-01 (ValidFrom <= the date AND (ValidTo blank OR ValidTo > the date)). ISO dates compare lexically. The single row that is TRUE for a given role names the agent active on the audit date — answerable only because history is retained.
schema.explanation_rich: **'As of March 1, 2026.'** Part IV makes this the headline reason to retain `filledBy` history. The witness is a pure validity-period containment: `ValidFrom <= 2026-03-01 < ValidTo` (with a blank `ValidTo` meaning still-open). For the VP-Engineering role this returns the prior holder, not the current one — exactly the answer that is impossible if the old triple was deleted.

schema.name: IsAgentTypeChange
schema.Description: TRUE iff this assignment changed the agent CLASS of the role (PriorFillerType set and different from FillerType). Part IV distinguishes a plain personnel/model swap (same class) from an agent-type transition, which carries compliance weight.

schema.name: RequiresComplianceAudit
schema.Description: Part IV: 'Changing the agent type of a step from ntwf:AIAgent to ntwf:HumanAgent ... is a data operation with compliance implications.' TRUE iff this assignment took a previously AI-executed binding and reassigned it to a human — the exact transition Part IV says the audit record must capture (when + why).
schema.explanation_rich: **The compliance-flagged transition.** Part IV singles out AIAgent -> HumanAgent reassignment as a data operation with compliance implications: a task that was automated is now done by a human, and the audit record must reflect when and why. This witness fires precisely on that direction, reading `PriorFillerType = AIAgent` and the current `FillerType = HumanAgent`, with `Reason` carrying the 'why'.

data.Reason: compliance reassignment: automated task assigned to a human for execution
model upgrade: returned to automated summarization (health-summarizer-v1.2.0)

The OWL transpiler mints each individual's IRI from this value (erb:<Iri>), so identity is path-derived and globally unique — no cross-table primary-key collisions.

HumanAgents.Description: Table: HumanAgents. The NTWF HumanAgent class — foaf:Person + prov:Agent. The only agent type permitted to fill roles whose step has requiresHumanApproval. Disjoint with AIAgent and AutomatedPipeline.

schema.name: RelativePath
schema.Description: Stable, DAG-derived location for this HumanAgent row. Root segment 'human-agents' + the row's primary key. No leading slash so the Iri swap is a clean 1:1 substitution. The relational analogue of a REST resource path; unique by construction across the whole model.

schema.name: Iri

The OWL transpiler mints each individual's IRI from this value (erb:<Iri>), so identity is path-derived and globally unique — no cross-table primary-key collisions.

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Full name of the person. Maps to foaf:name. Note: FOAF's name property is appropriate for persons, not for software systems (which use schema:name).

schema.name: Mbox
schema.Description: Email address of the person. Maps to foaf:mbox. Used for notifications and organizational directory integration.

schema.name: Roles
schema.Description: Back-reference to roles currently filled by this agent. Inverse of Roles.FilledByHumanAgent.

schema.name: RoleAssignments

AIAgents.Description: Table: AIAgents. The NTWF AIAgent class — prov:SoftwareAgent + ntwf:modelVersion. Distinguished from AutomatedPipeline by probabilistic (vs. deterministic) output semantics. Disjoint with HumanAgent and AutomatedPipeline. May never fill a role whose step has requiresHumanApproval.

schema.name: RelativePath
schema.Description: Stable, DAG-derived location for this AIAgent row. Root segment 'ai-agents' + the row's primary key. No leading slash so the Iri swap is a clean 1:1 substitution. The relational analogue of a REST resource path; unique by construction across the whole model.

schema.name: Iri

The OWL transpiler mints each individual's IRI from this value (erb:<Iri>), so identity is path-derived and globally unique — no cross-table primary-key collisions.

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Display name of the AI agent. Maps to schema:name (not foaf:name, which is for persons).

schema.name: Title
schema.Description: Descriptive title of the AI agent's function.

schema.name: ModelVersion
schema.Description: Version string of the AI model. Maps to ntwf:modelVersion. Makes AI-produced artifacts auditable at the version level. The domain declaration means this property applies only to AIAgent individuals.

schema.name: DeployedOn
schema.Description: Deployment date of this AI model version. Part IV (AI System Registry): 'risk-classifier-v2.4.1 was deployed on 2026-01-10'. Sourced from the AI system registry feed via the shared Dublin Core contract (dct:date).

schema.name: Roles
schema.Description: Back-reference to roles currently filled by this AI agent. Inverse of Roles.FilledByAIAgent.

schema.name: RoleAssignments
schema.Description: Back-reference to historical filledBy assignment periods filled by this AI agent. Inverse of RoleAssignments.FilledByAIAgent.

schema.name: AttributedArtifacts
schema.Description: Back-reference to WorkflowArtifacts attributed to this AI agent (prov:wasAttributedTo). Inverse of WorkflowArtifacts.AttributedToAIAgent. First leg of the Part IV 'blast radius' traversal.

schema.name: CountAttributedArtifacts
schema.Description: Part IV 'blast radius', leg 1: how many artifacts are attributed to this AI agent (prov:wasAttributedTo). Counts WorkflowArtifacts whose AttributedToAIAgent is this agent.
schema.explanation_rich: **Blast radius, leg 1.** Part IV asks: 'what is the blast radius of upgrading risk-classifier from v2.4.1 to v2.5.0?' The traversal starts here — every artifact attributed to the agent. `risk-classifier` produced the risk report, so a version bump's downstream reach begins from this count.

schema.name: CountImpactedWorkflows
schema.Description: Part IV 'blast radius', summarized: the number of distinct workflows reachable from this agent's attributed artifacts (each artifact is produced by a step that belongs to a workflow). With one workflow in the worked example, an upgrade to an agent that produced any artifact has a blast radius of 1 workflow. Counts artifacts attributed to this agent that resolve to a workflow.
schema.explanation_rich: **Blast radius, summarized to workflows.** Part IV's traversal ends at 'the workflows containing all of them.' Each attributed artifact resolves through its producing step to a workflow; this rollup counts how many workflow-bearing artifacts trace back to this agent — the operational answer to 'how far does upgrading this model reach?'

AutomatedPipelines.Description: Table: AutomatedPipelines. The NTWF AutomatedPipeline class — prov:SoftwareAgent + schema:SoftwareApplication. Distinguished from AIAgent by deterministic (vs. probabilistic) output semantics. Disjoint with HumanAgent and AIAgent. Carries schema:name, not foaf:name.

schema.name: RelativePath

The DerivedFromArtifact self-FK encodes the prov:wasDerivedFrom provenance chain; ProducedByStep maps prov:wasGeneratedBy; the AttributedTo* arms map prov:wasAttributedTo to the responsible agent.

schema.name: ParentPath
schema.Description: Helper: the WorkflowSteps parent's RelativePath, pulled across the ProducedByStep FK. Exists so RelativePath can concatenate the '/artifacts/' segment using only local-field '&' concat (the transpiler compiles a lookup as a pure passthrough, not a lookup+concat).

schema.name: RelativePath
schema.Description: Stable, DAG-derived location: this row nests under its WorkflowSteps parent. Concatenates the parent's path (ParentPath) with '/artifacts/' + this row's primary key. The DAG performs the recursion — one hop per table via ParentPath — so the full ancestry is encoded without a recursive formula. Unique by construction.

schema.name: Iri
schema.Description: Opaque stable identifier (the dash-form of RelativePath). Because RelativePath has no leading slash, this is a clean SUBSTITUTE of '/' for '-'. The OWL transpiler mints each individual's IRI from this value (erb:<Iri>), so identity is path-derived and globally unique — no cross-table primary-key collisions.

schema.name: Title
schema.Description: Human-readable artifact name. Maps to dct:title.

schema.name: Identifier
schema.Description: External system identifier. Maps to dct:identifier.

schema.name: ArtifactType
schema.Description: FK to the ArtifactTypeConcepts SKOS concept classifying this artifact. Maps to dct:type. Part IV: the CBox defines a concept scheme for artifact types.

schema.name: Created
schema.Description: Creation timestamp. Maps to dct:created.

schema.name: ProducedByStep
schema.Description: FK to the WorkflowStep that produced this artifact. Maps to prov:wasGeneratedBy. Inverse of WorkflowSteps.ProducesArtifacts.

schema.name: RequiredBySteps
schema.Description: Back-reference to the WorkflowStep(s) that consume this artifact as input (ntwf:requiresArtifact / prov:used). Inverse of WorkflowSteps.RequiresArtifacts.

schema.name: DerivedFromArtifact
schema.Description: Self-FK to the artifact this one was derived from. Maps to prov:wasDerivedFrom. Enables the full provenance chain query (CQ4).

schema.name: AttributedToHumanAgent
schema.Description: FK to HumanAgent responsible for this artifact. One arm of prov:wasAttributedTo (exactly one AttributedTo arm is set per artifact, mirroring the disjoint agent types).

schema.name: AttributedToAIAgent
schema.Description: FK to AIAgent responsible for this artifact. One arm of prov:wasAttributedTo.

schema.name: AttributedToAutomatedPipeline
schema.Description: FK to AutomatedPipeline responsible for this artifact. One arm of prov:wasAttributedTo.

schema.name: ProducingAgentType
schema.Description: Which disjoint agent class produced this artifact (HumanAgent / AIAgent / AutomatedPipeline), from whichever prov:wasAttributedTo arm is set. Lets CQ4 report which kind of agent each artifact in the lineage came from.

schema.name: HasDerivationParent
schema.Description: TRUE iff this artifact was derived from another (prov:wasDerivedFrom is set). Counting these across the chain gives CQ4's '4 derivation links among 5 artifacts' — every artifact except the first has a parent.

schema.name: ProducedByWorkflow
schema.Description: The workflow this artifact belongs to, resolved through ProducedByStep → WorkflowSteps.Workflow (artifact → producing step → workflow). Lets workflow-level rollups (e.g. CountDerivationLinks) aggregate artifacts without a redundant direct FK.

schema.name: HasProducingWorkflow
schema.Description: TRUE iff this artifact resolves to a producing workflow (ProducedByWorkflow is set). Lets the AIAgents blast-radius rollup (CountImpactedWorkflows) count only artifacts that reach a workflow, since COUNTIFS needs a boolean criterion column.

GovernanceRoles.Description: Table: GovernanceRoles. Part IV (Governance / Ownership) names two distinct ontology-governance roles: a Steward (responsible for the ontology's health — monitors drift, tracks external dependency updates, fields user questions, maintains docs, keeps the validation suite current; identifies that a change is needed but has no approval power) and an Authority (the power to approve changes to the CBox, ABox, and TBox; decides how and where a change is made; sits with the function that owns the domain). 'A steward who can make TBox or ABox changes without authority review is a single point of failure.' For an organization under 500 people a single person may hold both roles. This table models the maintenance discipline itself, as data, so the change log can attribute approvals to a named authority.

schema.name: RelativePath
schema.Description: Stable, DAG-derived location for this GovernanceRole row. Root segment 'governance-roles' + the row's primary key.

schema.name: Iri
schema.Description: Opaque stable identifier (the dash-form of RelativePath). The OWL transpiler mints each individual's IRI from this value.

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Slug form of the display name.

schema.name: DisplayName
schema.Description: Human-readable name of the governance role (e.g. 'Steward', 'Authority').

schema.name: Kind
schema.Description: Which of the two Part IV governance kinds this is: 'Steward' or 'Authority'.

schema.name: Responsibilities
schema.Description: What this role is responsible for, per Part IV. Steward: monitor drift, track external dependency updates, field user questions, maintain documentation, keep the validation suite current. Authority: approve changes to CBox/ABox/TBox; decide how and where a change is made.

schema.name: ApprovalScope
schema.Description: The boxes this role may approve changes to (CBox/ABox/TBox), or 'none' for a Steward — who can identify that a change is needed but cannot approve it.

schema.name: HeldBy
schema.Description: The person or function holding this role. Part IV: the steward is naturally whoever owns the engineering knowledge infrastructure; authority sits with the workflow governance function that owns the modeled domain. Under 500 people, one person may hold both.

schema.name: CanApproveChanges
schema.Description: TRUE iff this governance role carries approval power (Kind = 'Authority'). A Steward returns FALSE — Part IV: a steward making TBox/ABox changes without authority review is a single point of failure.

schema.name: ApprovedChanges
schema.Description: Back-reference to ChangeLog entries this governance role approved. Inverse of ChangeLog.ApprovedBy.

data.Responsibilities: Monitors the ontology for drift, tracks external-dependency updates (PROV-O, FOAF, Dublin Core, DCAT, Schema.org), fields questions from users, maintains documentation, and keeps the validation suite current. Identifies that a change is needed but does not approve it. Runs the periodic (at minimum quarterly) competency-question review: new unanswerable questions become scope-change requests, obsolete questions become deprecation candidates, and questions that return wrong answers are defects.

Holds the power to approve changes to the CBox, ABox, and TBox. Decides how and where a change is made. Reviews changes proposed by the steward so that no single person alters the knowledge graph's semantic integrity unchecked.

ChangeLog.Description: Table: ChangeLog. Part IV's minimum governance artifact: 'a change log that records every TBox and ABox modification, with its rationale.' Each entry records the four facts Part IV enumerates — the competency question that motivated the change, the terms affected, the version number of the release, and the date — plus the rationale and the Authority who approved it. Semantic-versioning discipline (MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH) is captured per entry via ChangeKind.

schema.name: RelativePath
schema.Description: Stable, DAG-derived location for this ChangeLog row. Root segment 'change-log' + the row's primary key.

schema.name: Iri
schema.Description: Opaque stable identifier (the dash-form of RelativePath).

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Human-readable label: the version and date of this change.

schema.name: Version
schema.Description: The release version number this change shipped in (semantic versioning MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH). Part IV: NTWF is currently at 1.1.0.

schema.name: ChangeDate
schema.Description: The date of the change. One of the four facts Part IV requires every change-log entry to record.

schema.name: ChangeKind
schema.Description: Semantic-versioning class of the change: 'patch' (documentation/label/comment only, formal model unchanged), 'minor' (additive — new classes/properties/CBox concepts, backward compatible), or 'major' (breaking — class removed/renamed, domain/range change invalidating ABox triples, or a new disjointness axiom).

schema.name: MotivatingQuestion
schema.Description: The competency question that motivated the change. One of the four facts Part IV requires. Empty if the change was driven by an external-dependency update rather than a CQ.

schema.name: TermsAffected
schema.Description: The ontology terms (classes/properties/concepts) the change added, removed, or modified. One of the four facts Part IV requires.

schema.name: Rationale
schema.Description: Why the change was made. Part IV requires every TBox/ABox modification to be logged with its rationale.

schema.name: ApprovedBy
schema.Description: FK to the GovernanceRole (an Authority) that approved this change. Part IV: changes to CBox/ABox/TBox require authority review; a steward identifying a need is not enough.

schema.name: IsBreakingChange
schema.Description: TRUE iff this is a major (breaking) change (ChangeKind = 'major') — Part IV: requires explicit update, re-validation, and migration planning for any system on the prior version.

schema.name: IsBackwardCompatible
schema.Description: TRUE iff systems on the prior version keep working against this release (ChangeKind is 'patch' or 'minor'). Part IV: patch and minor increments preserve backward compatibility; only major breaks it.

data.TermsAffected: Initial release: 9 classes, 13 object properties, 5 datatype properties, 2 CBox schemes.
data.Rationale: First validated release of the NTWF workflow ontology; passes the 104-test suite.

data.MotivatingQuestion: Which AI agent was executing this step on March 1, 2026?
data.TermsAffected: Added filledBy assignment history (RoleAssignments) with validity periods; AIAgent deployment date; agent-type-change audit witnesses.
data.Rationale: Operational governance needed retained filledBy history so historical 'as of date' and blast-radius queries are answerable without discarding provenance. Additive and backward compatible.

VocabularyReconciliations.Description: Table: VocabularyReconciliations. Part IV (External dependency change): when a borrowed term from a living standard (PROV-O, FOAF, Dublin Core, DCAT, Schema.org) is deprecated and re-homed into the NTWF namespace, the edit triggers a version bump and an owl:sameAs reconciliation relation declaring the old and new terms equivalent. The worked example: deprecating foaf:name, prepending the ntwf prefix to get ntwf:name, and asserting foaf:name owl:sameAs ntwf:name. Each row is one reconciliation, with the standard it came from and the version in which the reconciliation shipped.

schema.name: RelativePath
schema.Description: Stable, DAG-derived location for this reconciliation row. Root segment 'reconciliations' + the row's primary key.

schema.name: Iri
schema.Description: Opaque stable identifier (the dash-form of RelativePath).

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Human-readable label: the sameAs relation between the deprecated term and its NTWF replacement.

schema.name: DeprecatedTerm
schema.Description: The borrowed/deprecated term being reconciled (e.g. foaf:name).

schema.name: ReplacementTerm
schema.Description: The NTWF-namespaced replacement term (e.g. ntwf:name).

schema.name: ReconciliationRelation
schema.Description: The OWL relation asserting equivalence. Part IV uses owl:sameAs.

schema.name: SourceStandard
schema.Description: The external standard the deprecated term came from (PROV-O, FOAF, Dublin Core, DCAT, Schema.org).

schema.name: IntroducedInVersion
schema.Description: The NTWF release version in which this reconciliation shipped. Part IV: re-homing a term triggers a version bump.

schema.name: Rationale
schema.Description: Why the term was re-homed (e.g. upstream deprecation; semantic-alignment shift).

data.Rationale: Part IV worked example: a deprecated FOAF element is re-prefixed into the NTWF namespace; existing data stays valid (URIs do not disappear), and foaf:name owl:sameAs ntwf:name keeps the two interchangeable for reasoners.

Part IV external-dependency fact: DCAT went through a major v2 -> v3 revision that introduced new classes and deprecated others. NTWF tracks the alignment so its dcat:Dataset usage stays semantically current; existing data remains valid because URIs do not disappear.

schema.name: ScenarioId
schema.Description: Stable identifier for a curated demo scenario (a named set of raw-fact edits applied at once).

schema.name: RelativePath
schema.Description: DAG-derived location for this Scenario row: root segment 'scenarios' + the primary key.

schema.name: Iri
schema.Description: Opaque stable identifier (dash-form of RelativePath).

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Slug form of the human label.

schema.name: Label
schema.Description: Human-readable button label for this scenario in the picker.

schema.name: Icon
schema.Description: A single emoji shown beside the label in the picker.

schema.name: Explanation
schema.Description: Plain-language description of what this scenario changes and what the reasoner will derive as a result. Shown in the floating scenario picker so the user knows what each preset does before applying it.

schema.name: SortOrder
schema.Description: Display order in the picker (ascending).

schema.name: IsReset
schema.Description: True for the single 'restore baseline' scenario; the picker styles it as a secondary action.

schema.name: Edits
schema.Description: JSON-encoded ordered list of raw-fact assignments this scenario applies. Each item is {class, id|match, set:{field:value,...}} where 'class' is a rulebook table (camelCase keys on the raw store), 'id' targets a row by its *Id primary key (or 'match':'first' for the singleton Workflow), and 'set' is the raw fields to assign. The backend replays this list against the active raw store, then re-reasons — the scenario NEVER sets a derived field. This is the single source of truth for what each demo scenario does; the app's picker and the apply endpoint both read it from here (same JSON-on-a-first-class-table pattern as __meta__'s JsonValue).

data.Label: Stale workflow with an AI step
data.Explanation: Backdates the workflow's last review past its 12-month policy window so the docs go STALE. The release already has AI-executed steps, so stale-docs-AND-an-AI-step fires the article's headline business query: IsStaleAndHasAIAgent (CQ5 extended — stale workflows that involve AI agents).

data.Label: All-human release
data.Explanation: Reassigns every AI agent and pipeline to a human. The AI-step count drops to zero, so the compliance risk can no longer fire — even if the docs are stale. Shows the role→agent indirection: personnel change is one edge, not a rewrite.

data.Label: AI at the approval gate
data.Explanation: Hands the Legal Compliance Reviewer role — whose review step requires human sign-off — to an AI agent. The reasoner's consistency witness fires on that step: a requires-human-approval step is no longer human-filled. The rule break is DERIVED, not hand-checked.

data.Label: Reset to baseline
data.Explanation: Restores the canonical seed assignments and a fresh review date — the known-good starting point. Every role is filled by exactly one agent, no requires-human-approval step is AI-filled, and the workflow is within its staleness window, so all the article's clean-ABox witnesses read green.

__meta__.Description: Project-level metadata that travels with the rulebook: tagline, motif, narrative descriptions, substrate list, signature rows, etc. One row per metadata key. Use ValueType to interpret StringValue vs JsonValue.

schema.name: MetaKey
schema.Description: The metadata key (e.g. 'tagline', 'motif_palette', 'substrates'). Unique within the table.

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Identifier for this metadata entry. Mirrors MetaKey so the row is addressable by Name like every other table.

schema.name: ValueType
schema.Description: How to interpret the value columns: 'string' (use StringValue), 'object' (parse JsonValue as JSON object), 'array' (parse JsonValue as JSON array).

schema.name: StringValue
schema.Description: Plain string value. Populated when ValueType == 'string'; null otherwise.

schema.name: JsonValue
schema.Description: JSON-encoded value. Populated when ValueType == 'object' or 'array'; null when ValueType == 'string'.

data.Name: tagline
data.StringValue: One workflow, told tip to tail: humans, AI, and pipelines under one ontology.

data.Name: description_rich
data.StringValue: Talisman's Special Solutions models the **NTWF ontology** from Jessica Talisman's *Intentional Arrangement* series — the workflow language for a fictional 1,100-person technology company, *Talisman's Special Solutions*. Instead of a pile of tutorial stubs, it tells **one complete story**: the *Production Deployment Workflow*. Five ordered steps carry a release from AI risk assessment to post-deploy report, passing through all three disjoint agent types — an AI Risk Analysis Agent (RiskAnalysis-AI, `risk-classifier-v2.4.1`); a human Legal Compliance Reviewer (James Okafor); a human Release Manager (Maria Gonzalez), who owns the approval gate that authorizes the release; and an automated CI/CD pipeline. Roles are kept separate from the agents who fill them (Heuristic 2), so a personnel change is one edge, not a workflow rewrite. A delegation chain (Release Manager -> VP Engineering -> CTO) handles escalation; a PROV provenance chain links five artifacts from risk report to post-deploy report; and a DCAT dataset feeds the AI step. Every NTWF class, property, and competency question is exercised by real, connected rows — answered by derived columns and first-class transitive-closure views (`precedesStep`, `delegatesTo`), with load-bearing INDEX/MATCH lookups resolving the role-to-agent indirection.

data.Name: use_cases
data.JsonValue: ["**CQ1 — steps in order.** Walk `StepPrecedence` from `prod-deploy-step-1`; the four asserted edges (1->2->3->4->5) imply the full transitive ordering, including the never-asserted 1->5.", "**CQ2 — who approves a deployment.** From the *Release Approval Gate* (step 3) to its assigned role (Release Manager) to the human who fills it: Maria Gonzalez.", "**CQ3 — AI vs human steps.** Two steps are executed by AI agents — step 1 (AI Risk Assessment, RiskAnalysis-AI) and step 5 (Post-Deployment Health Report, DeploymentHealth-AI) — and two require a human decision (`RequiresHumanApproval`): step 2 (Legal Review) and step 3 (Release Approval Gate). The deterministic CI/CD pipeline runs step 4 — a third, disjoint agent type, not counted as AI.", "**CQ4 — artifact provenance.** Follow `DerivedFromArtifact` from the post-deploy report back through deployment log, release authorization, legal clearance, to the risk report — five artifacts, four derivation links, one chain.", "**CQ6 — escalation.** When the Release Manager is unavailable, traverse `DelegatesTo`: Release Manager -> VP Engineering -> CTO. Note an AI role has no DelegatesTo edge — it cannot stand in a human delegation chain.", "**CQ7 — cross-department.** The Production Deployment workflow spans Engineering (six roles) and Legal (one role) — answerable by intersecting `Roles.OwnedBy`; exactly one step (Legal Review) is Legal-owned.", "**CQ8 — datasets and the AI that read them.** The Q1 2026 Risk Metrics dataset is `ConsumedBySteps` step 1, whose role is filled by RiskAnalysis-AI.", "**Read your first calculated field.** `Workflows.Name` is just `DisplayName` lowercased with dashes — but it lives in the rulebook, not in code. Rename the workflow and the slug updates in every substrate.", "**Trace a second-order rule.** `HasMoreThan1Step` reads the rollup `CountOfNonProposedSteps`, which counts `WorkflowSteps`. Two hops — the whole DAG in miniature."]

data.Name: journal_seed
data.StringValue: One workflow pinned to the board — Production Deployment — with five steps in order, a human, an AI, a pipeline, a Release-Manager-owned approval gate, a delegation chain up to the CTO, and a provenance trail of five artifacts. The whole NTWF ontology, told as a single story.

data.Name: CMCC_Summary
data.StringValue: Rulebook-first project: Schemas, Data, Relationships (FK links), Aggregations (COUNTIFS/Rollups, including conditional counts over derived child fields), Lookups (INDEX/MATCH, load-bearing for role-to-agent chains), transitive-closure views, and Calculated fields (formulas) in Excel dialect. Field types are declared directly in the rulebook JSON; the build derives all substrates from the rulebook as the single source of truth.

data.Name: conversion_metadata
data.JsonValue: {"table_count": 14, "tool_version": "2.0.0", "field_type_mapping": "checkbox→boolean, number→number/integer, relationship→relationship, lookup→lookup, aggregation→aggregation, formula→calculated", "source": "rulebook-first", "type_inference": {"enabled": true, "priority": "rulebook_schema (NO COERCION) → formula_analysis → reference_resolution → data_analysis (last-resort inference; build SHOULD FAIL rather than silently use this)", "nullable_support": true, "error_value_handling": "#NUM!, #ERROR!, #N/A, #REF!, #DIV/0!, #VALUE!, #NAME? are treated as NULL"}}
